lesson: The # (h1) heading is Slide 1. The intro paragraph immediately below it is the slide body. <!-- Slide 1 --> goes after that paragraph, before the first --- separator. Miscounting here leads to slide total mismatches.
A short recap slide (≤2 min) bridging from the previous lecture's limitations works better than jumping straight into new syntax. It gives students a motivation frame before content starts.

The instructor's PDF uses questions as slide titles (e.g. 'What's the big idea?', 'That sounds like an if statement…'). Apply this question-as-hook pattern more consistently on introductory slides — it mirrors the source material's style and pulls students in.

When a lecture introduces multiple variants of the same concept (e.g. three loop types), end with a 'which one to use?' decision framework before the lab. Students need synthesis before application — skipping it produces guessing, not reasoning.

The intro paragraph under a topic's # heading should appear on a separate ## slide with the same title, not on the # slide itself. In Reveal.js the # slide is a vertical navigation parent — putting content there makes it easy to skip.

When a slide needs both a concept explanation and a code example, use two consecutive ## slides with the same title: first slide has the concept as bullets, second slide has the code. This is the implementation of the 'no text on both sides of code' rule.

Use ::: {.columns} / ::: {.column width="50%"} / ::: / ::: for two-column layout. Code blocks inside columns require ```{.cpp} (dot notation) not ```cpp. Close each column with ::: and the outer container with ::: (trailing space). Use for side-by-side code comparison and multi-choice scenario slides.

When you have a code example do not have text on the same page unless the example is very short (<6 lines). Even then, have text either above or below the code example but not both. It’s OK to add another slide if needed.
Prefer lists to blocks of text.

Opening slides should contain no prose paragraphs. The opening slide poses the question and stops — one short question headline plus at most one or two bullets. Any explanatory paragraph belongs on a subsequent slide.

Opening question titles should be headline-length fragments, not full interrogative sentences. 'Moving a loop through every element in a collection?' not 'How does a loop move through every element in a collection?'
Summary slides should use bullet lists, not prose paragraphs. Each bullet captures one takeaway. Drop trailing wrap-up sentences.
Remove trailing explanatory sentences after bullet lists. If the bullets are clear, the sentence restating their logic is filler.
Add dot-padded inline comments to code examples to narrate specific lines (e.g. //.......... outer loop). This turns code into annotated reading material instead of a block students have to decode.

For visual or structural concepts, show the concrete artifact first and let students process it before naming the concept. A bare 3x3 table posing the problem, then the answer on the next slide, is more effective than a text question.
Use nested sub-bullets for hierarchical content. Do not pack sub-items inline after an em-dash on the same bullet line.
Use # (h1) for Last Time and Today's Agenda slides so they become proper horizontal sections alongside topic columns, not vertical slides in an implicit section 0.
On code-heavy slides, explanatory bullets that annotate the code belong in speaker notes, not below the code block. Post-code bullets create text-on-both-sides violations and clutter the visual.

For topics that introduce a concept students already know in another form (e.g. arrays when students know vectors), open with an explicit contrast framing — 'same idea, different contract' — rather than a neutral definition. It orients students faster and reduces the feeling that they're starting from zero.

When a topic has a direct 'which one to use?' decision (e.g. index-based for loop vs. range-for), put the decision framework on its own slide near the end of the topic — after students have seen both options — rather than burying it in bullets on an earlier slide. Students need to see the options before they can reason about the choice.

When introducing an algorithm that modifies data in place (e.g. swap in bubble sort), show the broken version first — what happens if you skip the temp variable — before showing the correct version. The failure mode is more memorable than a rule stated up front.

When a topic has a pointer/value gotcha that will bite students immediately (e.g. array decay on function arguments), plant a one-slide preview of the problem in the preceding topic rather than waiting for the full explanation. Students encounter the surprise in a low-stakes context first, so the detailed explanation lands on already-primed minds.

Slide titles are labels, not sentence fragments. Do not append descriptors after an em-dash (e.g. 'Reading the Nth record — the formula' → 'Reading the Nth record'). The title names the slide; the content explains it.
